(French: [[ʒɑ̃ simeɔ̃ ʃaʁdɛ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); November 2, 1699 – December 6, 1779[[1]](#cite_note-1)) was an 18th-century [French](/wiki/France "France") [painter](/wiki/List_of_painters "List of painters").[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is considered a master of [still life](/wiki/Still_life "Still life"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and is also noted for his [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") paintings which depict kitchen maids, children, and domestic activities. Carefully balanced composition, soft diffusion of light, and granular [impasto](/wiki/Impasto "Impasto") characterize his work.

**Jean Siméon Chardin** (French: [[ʒɑ̃ simeɔ̃ ʃaʁdɛ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); November 2, 1699 – December 6, 1779[[1]](#cite_note-1)) was an 18th-century [French](/wiki/France "France") [painter](/wiki/List_of_painters "List of painters").[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is considered a master of [still life](/wiki/Still_life "Still life"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and is also noted for his [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") paintings which depict kitchen maids, children, and domestic activities. Carefully balanced composition, soft diffusion of light, and granular [impasto](/wiki/Impasto "Impasto") characterize his work.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Chardin was born in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), the son of a cabinetmaker, and rarely left the city. He lived on the [Left Bank](/wiki/Rive_Gauche "Rive Gauche") near [Saint-Sulpice](/wiki/%C3%89glise_Saint-Sulpice,_Paris "Église Saint-Sulpice, Paris") until 1757, when [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") granted him a studio and living quarters in the [Louvre Palace](/wiki/Louvre_Palace "Louvre Palace").[[4]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-4)

Chardin entered into a marriage contract with Marguerite Saintard in 1723, whom he did not marry until 1731.[[5]](#cite_note-rosenberg179-5) He served apprenticeships with the history painters [Pierre-Jacques Cazes](/wiki/Pierre-Jacques_Cazes "Pierre-Jacques Cazes") and [Noël-Nicolas Coypel](/wiki/No%C3%ABl-Nicolas_Coypel "Noël-Nicolas Coypel"), and in 1724 became a master in the [Académie de Saint-Luc](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_de_Saint-Luc "Académie de Saint-Luc").

According to one nineteenth-century writer, at a time when it was hard for unknown painters to come to the attention of the Royal Academy, he first found notice by displaying a painting at the "small Corpus Christi" (held eight days after the regular one) on the [Place Dauphine](/wiki/Place_Dauphine "Place Dauphine") (by the [Pont Neuf](/wiki/Pont_Neuf "Pont Neuf")). [Van Loo](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_van_Loo "Jean-Baptiste van Loo"), passing by in 1720, bought it and later assisted the young painter.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Self_Portrait_at_an_Easel_%28ca._1779%29%2C_pastel%2C_40.5_x_32.5_cm.%2C_Louvre.jpg/250px-Self_Portrait_at_an_Easel_%28ca._1779%29%2C_pastel%2C_40.5_x_32.5_cm.%2C_Louvre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Self_Portrait_at_an_Easel_(ca._1779),_pastel,_40.5_x_32.5_cm.,_Louvre.jpg)

*Self Portrait at an Easel* (ca. 1779), pastel, 40.5 x 32.5 cm., [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre")

Upon presentation of *[The Ray](/wiki/The_Ray_(Chardin) "The Ray (Chardin)")* and *[The Buffet](/wiki/The_Buffet_(Chardin) "The Buffet (Chardin)")* in 1728, he was admitted to the [Académie Royale de Peinture et de Sculpture](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_Royale_de_Peinture_et_de_Sculpture "Académie Royale de Peinture et de Sculpture").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The following year he ceded his position in the [Académie de Saint-Luc](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_de_Saint-Luc "Académie de Saint-Luc"). He made a modest living by "produc[ing] paintings in the various genres at whatever price his customers chose to pay him",[[8]](#cite_note-Rosenberg56-8) and by such work as the restoration of the [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") at the Galerie François I at [Fontainebleau](/wiki/Palace_of_Fontainebleau "Palace of Fontainebleau") in 1731.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
In November 1731 his son Jean-Pierre was baptized, and a daughter, Marguerite-Agnès, was baptized in 1733. In 1735 his wife Marguerite died, and within two years Marguerite-Agnès had died as well.[[5]](#cite_note-rosenberg179-5)

Beginning in 1737 Chardin exhibited regularly at the [Salon](/wiki/Salon_(Paris) "Salon (Paris)"). He would prove to be a "dedicated academician",[[4]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-4) regularly attending meetings for fifty years, and functioning successively as counsellor, treasurer, and secretary, overseeing in 1761 the installation of Salon exhibitions.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Jean-Baptiste_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_021.jpg/250px-Jean-Baptiste_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Baptiste_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_021.jpg)

*Françoise-Marguerite Pouget* (1707–1791), 2nd wife of Chardin (1775), pastel, 46 x 38 cm., [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre")

Chardin's work gained popularity through reproductive engravings of his [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") paintings (made by artists such as [François-Bernard Lépicié](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Bernard_L%C3%A9pici%C3%A9 "François-Bernard Lépicié") and P.-L. Sugurue), which brought Chardin income in the form of "what would now be called royalties".[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1744 he entered his second marriage, this time to Françoise-Marguerite Pouget. The union brought a substantial improvement in Chardin's financial circumstances. In 1745 a daughter, Angélique-Françoise, was born, but she died in 1746.

In 1752 Chardin was granted a pension of 500 [livres](/wiki/Livre_tournois "Livre tournois") by Louis XV. In 1756 Chardin returned to the subject of the still life. At the [Salon of 1759](/wiki/Salon_of_1759 "Salon of 1759") he exhibited nine paintings; it was the first Salon to be commented upon by [Denis Diderot](/wiki/Denis_Diderot "Denis Diderot"), who would prove to be a great admirer and public champion of Chardin's work.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Beginning in 1761, his responsibilities on behalf of the Salon, simultaneously arranging the exhibitions and acting as treasurer, resulted in a diminution of productivity in painting, and the showing of 'replicas' of previous works.[[13]](#cite_note-rosenberg183-13) In 1763 his services to the Académie were acknowledged with an extra 200 livres in pension. In 1765 he was unanimously elected associate member of the [Académie des sciences, belles-lettres et arts de Rouen](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_des_sciences,_belles-lettres_et_arts_de_Rouen "Académie des sciences, belles-lettres et arts de Rouen"), but there is no evidence that he left Paris to accept the honor.[[13]](#cite_note-rosenberg183-13) By 1770 Chardin was the 'Premier peintre du roi', and his pension of 1,400 livres was the highest in the academy.[[14]](#cite_note-rosenberg184-14) In the 1770s his eyesight weakened and he took to painting in [pastels](/wiki/Pastels "Pastels"), a medium in which he executed portraits of his wife and himself (see *Self-portrait* at top right). His works in pastels are now highly valued.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1772 Chardin's son, also a painter, drowned in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), a probable suicide.[[14]](#cite_note-rosenberg184-14) The artist's last known oil painting was dated 1776; his final Salon participation was in 1779, and featured several pastel studies. Gravely ill by November of that year, he died in Paris on December 6, at the age of 80.

## Work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Work")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Jean-Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_-_Jar_of_Apricots_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Jean-Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_-_Jar_of_Apricots_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin_-_Jar_of_Apricots_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Jar of Apricots* (1758), oil on canvas, 57 x 51 cm., [Art Gallery of Ontario](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Ontario "Art Gallery of Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/The_Sliced_Melon_%281763%29%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_60_x_52_cm%2C_Louvre.jpg/250px-The_Sliced_Melon_%281763%29%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_60_x_52_cm%2C_Louvre.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Sliced_Melon_(1763),_oil_on_canvas,_60_x_52_cm,_Louvre.jpg)

*The Sliced Melon* (1760), oil on canvas, 57 x 52 cm., [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre")

Chardin worked very slowly and painted only slightly more than 200 pictures (about four a year) in total.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Chardin's work had little in common with the [Rococo painting](/wiki/Rococo_painting "Rococo painting") that dominated French art in the 18th century. At a time when history painting was considered the supreme classification for public art, Chardin's subjects of choice were viewed as minor categories.[[4]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-4) He favored simple yet beautifully textured still lifes, and sensitively handled domestic interiors and [genre paintings](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works"). Simple, even stark, paintings of common household items (*Still Life with a Smoker's Box*) and an uncanny ability to portray children's innocence in an unsentimental manner (*Boy with a Top* [right]) nevertheless found an appreciative audience in his time, and account for his timeless appeal.

Largely self-taught, Chardin was greatly influenced by the realism and subject matter of the 17th-century [Low Country](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") masters. Despite his unconventional portrayal of the ascendant [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"), early support came from patrons in the French [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy "Aristocracy"), including Louis XV. Though his popularity rested initially on paintings of animals and fruit, by the 1730s he introduced kitchen utensils into his work (*The Copper Cistern*, c. 1735, Louvre). Soon figures populated his scenes as well, supposedly in response to a portrait painter who challenged him to take up the genre.[[17]](#cite_note-17) *Woman Sealing a Letter* (ca. 1733), which may have been his first attempt,[[18]](#cite_note-18) was followed by half-length compositions of children saying grace, as in *[Le Bénédicité](/wiki/Le_B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dicit%C3%A9 "Le Bénédicité")*, and kitchen maids in moments of reflection. These humble scenes deal with simple, everyday activities, yet they also have functioned as a source of documentary information about a level of French society not hitherto considered a worthy subject for painting.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The pictures are noteworthy for their formal structure and pictorial harmony.[[4]](#cite_note-metmuseum1-4) Chardin said about painting, "Who said one paints with colors? One *employs* colors, but one paints with *feeling*."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

A child playing was a favourite subject of Chardin. He depicted an adolescent building a house of cards on at least four occasions. The version at [Waddesdon Manor](/wiki/Waddesdon_Manor "Waddesdon Manor") is the most elaborate. Scenes such as these derived from 17th-century Netherlandish vanitas works, which bore messages about the transitory nature of human life and the worthlessness of material ambitions, but Chardin's also display a delight in the ephemeral phases of childhood for their own sake.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Chardin frequently painted replicas of his compositions—especially his genre paintings, nearly all of which exist in multiple versions which in many cases are virtually indistinguishable.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Beginning with *The Governess* (1739, in the [National Gallery of Canada](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Canada "National Gallery of Canada"), [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa")), Chardin shifted his attention from working-class subjects to slightly more spacious scenes of bourgeois life.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Chardin's extant paintings, which number about 200,[[8]](#cite_note-Rosenberg56-8) are in many major museums, including the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre").

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Souvenir_MET_DP169562.jpg/250px-Souvenir_MET_DP169562.jpg)](/wiki/File:Souvenir_MET_DP169562.jpg)

Enameled box and other objects painted after the style of Chardin

Chardin's influence on the art of the modern era was wide-ranging and has been well-documented.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet")'s half-length *Boy Blowing Bubbles* and the still lifes of [Paul Cézanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne") are equally indebted to their predecessor.[[25]](#cite_note-wilkin1-25) He was one of [Henri Matisse](/wiki/Henri_Matisse "Henri Matisse")'s most admired painters; as an art student Matisse made copies of four Chardin paintings in the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre").[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Chaïm Soutine](/wiki/Cha%C3%AFm_Soutine "Chaïm Soutine")'s still lifes looked to Chardin for inspiration, as did the paintings of [Georges Braque](/wiki/Georges_Braque "Georges Braque"), and later, [Giorgio Morandi](/wiki/Giorgio_Morandi "Giorgio Morandi").[[25]](#cite_note-wilkin1-25) In 1999 [Lucian Freud](/wiki/Lucian_Freud "Lucian Freud") painted and etched several copies after *The Young Schoolmistress* ([National Gallery, London](/wiki/National_Gallery,_London "National Gallery, London")).[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[Marcel Proust](/wiki/Marcel_Proust "Marcel Proust"), in the chapter "How to open your eyes?" from *[In Search of Lost Time](/wiki/In_Search_of_Lost_Time "In Search of Lost Time")* (*À la recherche du temps perdu*), describes a melancholic young man sitting at his simple breakfast table. The only comfort he finds is in the imaginary ideas of beauty depicted in the great masterpieces of the Louvre, materializing fancy palaces, rich princes, and the like. The author tells the young man to follow him to another section of the Louvre where the pictures of Chardin are. There he would see the beauty in still life at home and in everyday activities like peeling turnips.
